description:
You are a system administrator who has just rotated yesterday’s access log.  
A plain-text file already exists on the machine:

/home/user/server_access.log

Each line of this file contains exactly one host name (no leading or trailing
spaces, no additional columns).  Your goal is to compute how many times each
host name appears in that file and to write the result to a new report file.

Required result file:
  • Path: /home/user/server_report.txt  
  • Permissions: leave at the default the shell gives you (the tests only need
    to read it).  

Required format of /home/user/server_report.txt:
  • One line per distinct host name.  
  • Each line must contain two fields separated by a single space:
        COUNT HOSTNAME
    where COUNT is the decimal frequency of the host name.  
  • Lines must be sorted primarily by COUNT in descending numeric order.  
    If two or more hosts share the same COUNT, sort those hosts
    lexicographically in ascending order (case-sensitive).  
  • Do not include any extra header or footer lines.  
  • A final newline (line-feed) after the last line is mandatory.

Example of the exact expected layout (values here are illustrative only):
3 serverA
2 serverB
1 serverC⏎   ← final newline

You may use any shell tools you like, but the classic
“sort | uniq -c | sort” pipeline is sufficient.

After generating /home/user/server_report.txt, you are done; no additional
output is required.  The automated grader will read the file directly and
validate that it matches the specification above, down to whitespace and the
trailing newline.
truth:
Pre-existing file (available before the task starts)
---------------------------------------------------
Path: /home/user/server_access.log  
Contents (9 lines, each terminated by a single newline):
server1
server2
server1
server3
server2
server2
server1
server4
server3

Expected file to be created by the agent
----------------------------------------
Path: /home/user/server_report.txt  
Exact required contents (including final newline):
3 server1
3 server2
2 server3
1 server4

The file must contain nothing else—no extra spaces, empty lines, or headers.
